---
title: "Sources, loads, and resistance"
description: "Predict the operating voltage and current of a real source–load pair, including resistance, loading, tolerances, ratings, and measurement intrusion."
chapter-id: F03
status: draft
---

::: {.callout-note title="Chapter maturity — draft"}
This chapter presents a complete instructional path: first-principles derivations,
worked examples, an executable check, explicitly synthetic diagnostic data, a real
component-datasheet case, a bounded design decision, and a full exercise set. It is
still subject to technical and editorial revision. Physical evidence belongs in
the aligned laboratory; the synthetic values here do not qualify a physical claim.
See the [reading roadmap](../roadmap.qmd) for the meaning of status levels.
:::

::: {.callout-warning title="Safety boundary for this chapter"}
Any practical work discussed here is limited to **current-limited,
extra-low-voltage** sources, under local supervision and an approved procedure.
An open-circuit voltage does not reveal all the energy a source can deliver, and a
small internal resistance can permit destructive fault current. Never determine a
source's resistance by deliberately shorting an unknown source. This chapter is
**not** authorization for work on mains, high-voltage supplies, high-energy cells
or battery packs, RF power, or safety-critical equipment. Apply the source, path,
and target controls established in [F01](f01-safe-practice.qmd) [@iec61010].
:::

## Central question

> Why does the voltage of a source change when a real load is connected?

Consider a **hypothetical, illustrative observation**. A voltmeter reads 5.00 V
across an unloaded bench supply. Connect one approved load and the display at the
load reads 4.98 V. Connect a heavier load and it reads 4.2 V, or perhaps exactly
2.0 V while a **CC** indicator lights. Which statement is right: “the source is
5 V,” “the load pulled it down,” or “the source has become a 2 V source”? These
numbers motivate the question; they are not presented as measured evidence.

Each statement is incomplete because voltage and current are not properties of one
component in isolation. The connected source and load settle at one **operating
point**: a terminal voltage and current that satisfy both devices at once. The
source determines what voltage it can sustain as current changes; the load
determines what current it takes as voltage changes. Their intersection is what a
meter can observe.

::: {.callout-tip title="Predict before connecting"}
Consider an **illustrative** 5.00 V source represented, over a limited range, by an
ideal 5.00 V source in series with 47 Ω. Predict the load voltage for (a) no load,
(b) 1.0 kΩ, and (c) 47 Ω. Will any loaded voltage exceed 5.00 V? At what load will
the source lose half its open-circuit voltage? Commit to directional and
order-of-magnitude answers before doing the arithmetic. We reconcile them with a
schematic, equations, code, and synthetic diagnostic data later.
:::

## Learning outcomes

This chapter assumes [F02](f02-charge-voltage-current.qmd), especially potential
difference, current, nodes, ideal wires, and declared reference directions, plus
the algebra, ratios, and SI prefixes of
[M01](../appendices/m01-algebra-units-complex.qmd). After completing it you should
be able to:

- **distinguish an ideal voltage source, an ideal current source, a real source,
  and a load**, and explain why “source” and “load” describe a port interaction,
  not an immutable component type;
- **derive terminal resistance from material behavior**, distinguish resistance
  from resistivity and conductance, and state when Ohm's law is a useful linear
  constitutive relation rather than a universal law;
- **find the DC operating point** of a resistive load driven by a source with
  finite source resistance, interpret limiting cases, and recognize current limit
  or compliance as a change of operating region;
- **reduce series and parallel resistor combinations**, derive unloaded and
  loaded voltage-divider relations, and carry component tolerances far enough to
  bound a result;
- **predict voltmeter and ammeter intrusion**, use loaded measurements to estimate
  source resistance without shorting the source, and distinguish a source fault
  from an instrument-created discrepancy; and
- **decode common resistor markings and make a bounded selection** using nominal
  value, tolerance, temperature coefficient, dissipation, voltage, and
  environmental limits rather than resistance alone.

## A connected load reveals what an open circuit hides

The physical circuit, schematic, equations, and instrument reading describe the
same event at different resolutions. Begin at the two terminals shared by source
and load. Name the upper terminal A and lower terminal B. Define the terminal
voltage

$$
V \equiv V_{AB}=V_A-V_B,
$$

with $+$ at A and $-$ at B. Define the loop current $I$ as leaving the source at A,
entering the load at A, and returning through B. All quantities in this chapter are
steady DC values unless a different condition is stated. With that declaration,
positive $VI$ means the **load absorbs** power. The current entering the source's
positive terminal is $-I$, so its absorbed terminal power is $-VI$: for positive
$V$ and $I$, it delivers the same amount. [F05](f05-energy-power-thermal.qmd)
develops the complete power and heat account.

An **ideal voltage source** is a constitutive idealization that imposes
$V=V_S$ for any $I$. It has zero source resistance and unlimited current capability.
An **ideal current source** imposes $I=I_S$ for any $V$. It has infinite output
resistance and unlimited voltage capability. Neither unlimited capability is
physical. The ideal sources are useful pieces inside bounded descriptions of real
devices, not claims that a bench supply, cell, sensor output, or transistor can
ignore energy and thermal limits [@sedra2020microelectronic].

A **load** is whatever is connected to a port and draws energy under the stated
references and operating condition. A source need not be a battery: a powered
amplifier output, regulator, solar cell, or another subcircuit can supply energy.
Nor is a physical device always one or the other. A rechargeable cell is a source
while discharging and a load while charging. Under the passive sign convention of
F02, the sign of terminal power distinguishes the present role more reliably than
the component's name.

The practical question is therefore not “what is the source voltage?” but:

> At what $(V,I)$ pair do the source's terminal relation and the load's terminal
> relation both hold?

That pair is the operating point. It changes when either relation changes.

## Resistance connects field behavior to terminal behavior

F02 developed the local, low-field constitutive relation
$\vec J=\sigma\vec E$ for a homogeneous, isotropic, approximately ohmic material
at a stable temperature. Resistivity is defined as $\rho\equiv1/\sigma$. We can now
derive a terminal relation for a uniform specimen rather than memorize one.

Take a straight specimen of length $L$ and uniform cross-sectional area $A$.
Choose the $+x$ axis from terminal A to terminal B. Declare $V=V_A-V_B$ and current
$I$ positive from A to B, so current enters the terminal marked $+$. Let $E_x$ and
$J_x$ be the signed $x$ components of electric field and current density. Assume
they are approximately uniform, fringing and contact resistance are negligible,
material properties do not vary along the specimen, and temperature is stable.
The electrostatic potential relation from F02 and the definition of current density
then give

$$
V_A-V_B=\int_A^B \vec E\mathbin{\cdot}\mathrm d\vec l=E_xL,
\qquad
I=J_xA,
\qquad
J_x=\sigma E_x=\frac{E_x}{\rho}.
$$

Eliminating $E_x$ and $J_x$ gives

$$
V=\left(\rho\frac{L}{A}\right)I.
$$

Define the specimen's terminal resistance under these conditions as

$$
R\equiv\rho\frac{L}{A},
\qquad\text{so that}\qquad
V=RI.
$$ {#eq-ohm}

The dimensional check is

$$
[R]=[\rho]\frac{[L]}{[A]}
=(\Omega\!\cdot\!\text{m})\frac{\text{m}}{\text{m}^2}=\Omega,
\qquad
[RI]=\Omega\!\cdot\!\text{A}=\text{V}.
$$

Equation @eq-ohm is **Ohm's law for an ohmic element**: a linear constitutive
relation within the operating range in which $R$ is effectively constant. It is
not a definition that forces every device to be linear. A diode, lamp filament,
thermistor, battery, and transistor can all have curved current–voltage relations.
At an operating point with $I\ne0$, one may calculate the **static resistance**
$R_\text{static}=V/I$, while the local slope defines the **differential
resistance** $r=\mathrm dV/\mathrm dI$. They need not be equal. Only a straight
line through the origin has one constant resistance describing every point.

The siemens (S) is the reciprocal ohm. **Conductance** is defined for a linear
resistor as

$$
G\equiv\frac{1}{R},
\qquad I=GV,
\qquad [G]=\text{S}=\Omega^{-1}=\text{A/V}.
$$ {#eq-conductance}

Conductance is often the natural quantity for parallel paths because their
currents add.

::: {.callout-note title="Demonstration — geometry, sign, and limits"}
Use an illustrative material with
$\rho=1.7\times10^{-8}~\Omega\!\cdot\!\text{m}$, length $L=2.0$ m, and area
$A=0.50~\text{mm}^2=5.0\times10^{-7}$ m². The terminal resistance estimate is

$$
R=\rho\frac{L}{A}
=(1.7\times10^{-8})\frac{2.0}{5.0\times10^{-7}}
=6.8\times10^{-2}~\Omega.
$$

At $I=+1.0$ A from A to B, $V_{AB}=+68$ mV. If the same physical current flows
from B to A while the declared references stay fixed, $I=-1.0$ A and
$V_{AB}=-68$ mV. Doubling $L$ doubles $R$; doubling $A$ halves it; taking
$\rho\to0$ recovers the ideal-wire limit. Contact resistance, self-heating, a
changing $\rho(T)$, or nonuniform geometry would make this estimate incomplete.
The numerical resistivity is illustrative here; use a material and temperature
specification for a design.
:::

## A finite source resistance creates voltage sag

Many real voltage-producing devices can be described near a bounded operating
region by an ideal open-circuit voltage $V_\text{OC}$ in series with a source
resistance $R_S$. This is a **first-order linear approximation**, not a statement
about the device's internal construction. Electrochemical polarization, regulator
control, current limiting, wiring, connectors, temperature, state of charge, and
frequency can all make the apparent $R_S$ change.

Use the references already declared: $I$ leaves terminal A and $V=V_A-V_B$.
Traversing from the ideal source to terminal A, the resistor drops $IR_S$, so the
source terminal relation is

$$
V=V_\text{OC}-IR_S.
$$ {#eq-source-line}

This straight line is the source's **terminal characteristic**. At $I=0$ it gives
$V=V_\text{OC}$, as required. Extrapolating it to $V=0$ gives
$I=V_\text{OC}/R_S$, but that is a **predicted short-circuit current of the linear
approximation**, not permission to perform a short-circuit test. A real source may
current-limit, heat, fail, arc, or deliver dangerous energy before that point.

Connect a linear load $R_L$ between A and B. With current entering the load's
positive terminal, its relation is

$$
V=IR_L.
$$ {#eq-load-line}

The operating point satisfies both @eq-source-line and @eq-load-line. Equating
them and solving gives

$$
I=\frac{V_\text{OC}}{R_S+R_L},
\qquad
V=\frac{V_\text{OC}R_L}{R_S+R_L}.
$$ {#eq-source-load}

On a graph with $I$ increasing to the right and $V$ increasing upward, the source
characteristic begins at $(0,V_\text{OC})$ and falls with slope $-R_S$; the
resistive load line begins at the origin and rises with slope $R_L$. Their one
intersection is @eq-source-load. A smaller $R_L$ makes the load line shallower and
moves the intersection toward greater current and lower voltage. This graphical
language becomes especially valuable when either characteristic is nonlinear.

![A real voltage source and resistive load with synchronized references. The loop current $I$ leaves the source's positive side, crosses $R_S$, enters the load $R_L$ at its positive terminal, and returns to 0 V. The load voltage $V=V_A-V_B$ is measured at TP1 relative to TP0.](../../../build/circuits/f03-source-load.png){#fig-source-load fig-alt="An ideal open-circuit voltage source V_OC in series with source resistance R_S drives load resistor R_L. Current I points from the source through R_S into R_L. Test point TP1 marks the positive load terminal A and TP0 marks terminal B at zero volts; voltage V is positive at A."}

The dimensions are explicit: volts divided by ohms gives amperes, and the voltage
ratio $R_L/(R_S+R_L)$ is dimensionless. Three limiting cases make the physics
memorable:

- $R_L\to\infty$: $I\to0$ and $V\to V_\text{OC}$. An ideal voltmeter approaches
  this open-circuit observation.
- $R_L=R_S$: $V=V_\text{OC}/2$. Half the open-circuit voltage appears at the load.
- $R_L\to0$: the linear relation predicts $V\to0$ and
  $I\to V_\text{OC}/R_S$. This is where current limit, heating, and other omitted
  physics are most likely to invalidate the approximation.

The fractional voltage sag relative to open circuit is

$$
\frac{V_\text{OC}-V}{V_\text{OC}}
=\frac{R_S}{R_S+R_L}.
$$ {#eq-sag}

Thus a source is **stiff** for a particular load only when $R_S\ll R_L$. “A stiff
5 V source” is incomplete without a load range and an allowed sag.

::: {.callout-note title="Worked example — settle the opening prediction"}
For the illustrative $V_\text{OC}=5.00$ V and $R_S=47~\Omega$ source:

- open circuit gives $I=0$ and $V=5.00$ V;
- with $R_L=1.0$ kΩ,
  $I=5.00/(47+1000)=4.78$ mA and
  $V=(4.78~\text{mA})(1.0~\text{k}\Omega)=4.78$ V; and
- with $R_L=47~\Omega$, the equal-resistance limit gives
  $I=5.00/(94~\Omega)=53.2$ mA and $V=2.50$ V.

No passive positive resistance in this circuit can make the loaded terminal
voltage exceed $V_\text{OC}$: @eq-source-line subtracts the nonnegative drop
$IR_S$. A reading above 5.00 V would therefore falsify at least one assumption —
perhaps the reference polarity, the “passive load” description, the open-circuit
value, or the constant-$R_S$ approximation.
:::

### Two safe loaded measurements estimate the slope

Equation @eq-source-line also gives a diagnostic method. Measure two steady
operating points $(V_1,I_1)$ and $(V_2,I_2)$ inside the same linear region, with
the same polarity and current reference. Subtraction eliminates $V_\text{OC}$:

$$
V_1-V_2=(I_2-I_1)R_S,
\qquad
R_S=\frac{V_1-V_2}{I_2-I_1}.
$$ {#eq-source-resistance}

Choose two approved loads that keep voltage, current, component dissipation, and
source energy inside their limits. Do **not** use a short circuit as the second
point. Once $R_S$ is estimated,
$V_\text{OC}=V_1+I_1R_S$. Because this is a slope estimate, meter uncertainty and
load tolerance matter strongly if the two currents are too close. For an ohmic,
characterized load, measuring its terminal voltage and calculating $I=V/R_L$ can
avoid inserting an ammeter; otherwise include the ammeter's burden. More than two
safe load points can reveal curvature or a current-limit knee that a two-point
slope would hide.

## Current sources face the dual limit: compliance

Some ports are better approximated by a current source. Keep $V=V_A-V_B$ across
the load and $I$ positive into its A terminal. A useful first-order description is
an ideal current $I_S$ in parallel with a finite output resistance $R_O$. The
source current divides between $R_O$ and $R_L$. Charge conservation at terminal A
gives

$$
I_S=\frac{V}{R_O}+\frac{V}{R_L}.
$$

Therefore

$$
V=I_S(R_O\parallel R_L),
\qquad
I=\frac{V}{R_L}
=I_S\frac{R_O}{R_O+R_L}.
$$ {#eq-current-source-load}

Here $R_O\parallel R_L$ denotes their parallel equivalent, derived in the next
section. As $R_O\to\infty$, $I\to I_S$: a good current source has output
resistance much larger than the load. But holding $I_S$ through a larger $R_L$
requires $V\approx I_SR_L$. A physical source reaches a maximum terminal voltage,
called its **compliance-voltage limit**, and can no longer maintain the programmed
current. An ideal current source connected to an open circuit would require
unbounded voltage; the resulting infinity is a warning about the idealization, not
a physical prediction.

Voltage-source current limit and current-source voltage compliance express the
same engineering truth: a real source can regulate one terminal quantity only
inside a bounded region of the $(V,I)$ plane.

## Series paths add resistance; parallel paths add conductance

Resistor reductions follow from the same terminal relations plus conservation.
They are exact for ideal linear resistors connected by ideal wires in a lumped
network. Lead, contact, leakage, temperature, and frequency effects determine how
well a physical assembly matches that description.

**Series.** Declare current $I$ positive through $n$ resistors in sequence, and
mark each resistor voltage $V_k$ positive where that current enters. The same
current crosses every element in steady state. If the intervening node potentials
are $V_0,V_1,\ldots,V_n$, the element differences telescope exactly in the
electrostatic lumped description:

$$
V_0-V_n=(V_0-V_1)+(V_1-V_2)+\cdots+(V_{n-1}-V_n)
=\sum_{k=1}^{n}V_k.
$$

Using $V_k=IR_k$ therefore gives

$$
V=\sum_{k=1}^{n}V_k
=I\sum_{k=1}^{n}R_k.
$$

Comparing with $V=IR_\text{series}$ gives

$$
R_\text{series}=\sum_{k=1}^{n}R_k.
$$ {#eq-series}

The result also follows dimensionally: only quantities with unit ohm are added.
If one positive resistance tends to zero, it becomes an ideal wire and drops out;
if one tends to infinity, it opens the path and the series current tends to zero
for finite applied voltage.

**Parallel.** Connect $n$ resistors across the same nodes A and B. Declare
$V=V_A-V_B$ and each branch current $I_k$ positive from A to B. Every branch has
the same voltage. Charge conservation at the junction, with negligible steady
accumulation as established in F02, makes the total current the branch sum:

$$
I=\sum_{k=1}^{n}I_k
=V\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{1}{R_k}
=V\sum_{k=1}^{n}G_k.
$$

Thus

$$
G_\text{parallel}=\sum_{k=1}^{n}G_k,
\qquad
\frac{1}{R_\text{parallel}}
=\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{1}{R_k}.
$$ {#eq-parallel}

For two resistors,

$$
R_1\parallel R_2
=\frac{R_1R_2}{R_1+R_2}.
$$ {#eq-two-parallel}

A parallel equivalent must be smaller than the smallest positive branch
resistance because it provides an additional current path. Two equal resistors
$R$ give $R/2$. If one branch tends to infinity it contributes zero conductance;
if one tends to zero, the combination tends to zero.

::: {.callout-note title="Worked example — reduce, check, and bound"}
Let $R_1=2.2$ kΩ be in series with the parallel pair
$R_2=3.3$ kΩ and $R_3=6.8$ kΩ. Then

$$
R_2\parallel R_3
=\frac{(3.3)(6.8)}{3.3+6.8}~\text{k}\Omega
=2.22~\text{k}\Omega,
$$

and $R_\text{eq}=2.2+2.22=4.42$ kΩ. The parallel result is correctly below
3.3 kΩ. If all three are independent ±5 % parts and only guaranteed limits are
known, monotonicity gives a conservative worst case by putting all values at the
same end: $R_\text{eq,min}=0.95(4.42)=4.20$ kΩ and
$R_\text{eq,max}=1.05(4.42)=4.64$ kΩ. That bound is not a statistical
uncertainty; it is a tolerance interval under the stated assumptions.
:::

## A divider is a source whose output resistance matters

Two series resistors create a fraction of an input voltage, but the familiar
ratio is the **unloaded** result. In @fig-voltage-divider, the ideal input source
sets $V_S$ relative to 0 V. $R_1$ connects the source to output node TP1; $R_2$
and the external load $R_L$ connect TP1 to 0 V. Define
$V_\text{out}=V_\text{TP1}-V_\text{TP0}$, with
$V_\text{TP0}=0$ V.

![A loaded voltage divider. $R_1$ feeds TP1 from source test point TP\_S; $R_2$ and the external load $R_L$ both return from TP1 to 0 V, so they are in parallel. The input is measured from TP\_S to TP0 and the output from TP1 to TP0.](../../../build/circuits/f03-voltage-divider.png){#fig-voltage-divider fig-alt="An ideal voltage source V_S drives source test point TP_S and resistor R1 to output test point TP1. Resistor R2 and load resistor R_L connect in parallel from TP1 to the zero-volt return at TP0. The output voltage is positive at TP1."}

With no external load ($R_L\to\infty$), the same current flows through $R_1$ and
$R_2$:

$$
I_0=\frac{V_S}{R_1+R_2},
\qquad
V_{\text{out},0}=I_0R_2
=V_S\frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}.
$$ {#eq-divider}

When $R_L$ is connected, the lower leg becomes
$R_P=R_2\parallel R_L$. The loaded output is therefore

$$
V_\text{out}=V_S\frac{R_P}{R_1+R_P},
\qquad R_P=R_2\parallel R_L.
$$ {#eq-loaded-divider}

The load always lowers the output for positive finite resistances because
$R_P<R_2$. This is not the load mysteriously “taking voltage”; it changes the
terminal relation, so the source and load settle at a new operating point.

There is a particularly revealing rewrite. Define the divider's open-circuit
output resistance as

$$
R_\text{out}=R_1\parallel R_2.
$$

Direct algebra applied to @eq-loaded-divider gives

$$
V_\text{out}
=V_{\text{out},0}\frac{R_L}{R_\text{out}+R_L}.
$$ {#eq-divider-loading}

Thus the whole divider, viewed from TP1, behaves for this calculation like a
voltage source $V_{\text{out},0}$ with series resistance $R_\text{out}$. F04
generalizes this observation into Thévenin and Norton network equivalents.

::: {.callout-note title="Worked example — a meter becomes the load"}
A 9.00 V ideal source drives $R_1=R_2=1.00$ MΩ. Unloaded,
$V_{\text{out},0}=4.50$ V and
$R_\text{out}=1.00~\text{M}\Omega\parallel1.00~\text{M}\Omega=500$ kΩ.
Connect a voltmeter whose specified DC input resistance is 10.0 MΩ. The meter is
$R_L$, so

$$
V_\text{read}=4.50~\text{V}
\frac{10.0~\text{M}\Omega}{10.0~\text{M}\Omega+0.500~\text{M}\Omega}
=4.286~\text{V}.
$$

The intrusion error relative to the unloaded quantity is

$$
\frac{V_\text{read}-V_{\text{out},0}}{V_{\text{out},0}}
=-4.76\%.
$$

The display may resolve millivolts while the measurement is wrong by 214 mV for
the intended measurand. If $R_L\to\infty$, the error tends to zero. A meter with
input resistance equal to $R_\text{out}$ would read exactly half the unloaded
voltage.
:::

## Instruments join the source–load system

A voltmeter is intended to approximate an open circuit; a current meter is
intended to approximate a short section of wire. Real instruments do neither
perfectly.

For a DC voltage measurement, use the meter's specified input resistance
$R_\text{in}$ at the selected function and range. It appears in parallel with the
circuit at the probe points. If the unmeasured port has open-circuit voltage
$V_0$ and output resistance $R_\text{out}$, @eq-divider-loading applies directly:

$$
V_\text{read}=V_0\frac{R_\text{in}}{R_\text{out}+R_\text{in}}.
$$ {#eq-voltmeter-loading}

To keep the magnitude of loading error below a fractional requirement
$\varepsilon$,

$$
\frac{R_\text{out}}{R_\text{out}+R_\text{in}}<\varepsilon
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
R_\text{in}>R_\text{out}\frac{1-\varepsilon}{\varepsilon}.
$$ {#eq-meter-requirement}

For 1 % maximum loading, $R_\text{in}>99R_\text{out}$. The casual “ten times
higher is enough” rule permits about 9.1 % error, not 1 %.

A real ammeter inserts a nonzero **burden resistance** $R_A$ (and sometimes an
additional burden voltage from its measurement circuit). For a source
$V_\text{OC}$, source resistance $R_S$, and load $R_L$, the desired current before
insertion is $I_0=V_\text{OC}/(R_S+R_L)$. If the meter is represented by series
$R_A$, it reads

$$
I_\text{read}=\frac{V_\text{OC}}{R_S+R_L+R_A},
\qquad
\frac{I_\text{read}-I_0}{I_0}
=-\frac{R_A}{R_S+R_L+R_A}.
$$ {#eq-ammeter-loading}

The current range matters because different shunts can produce different burdens.
Never place an ammeter directly across a source: that asks its low resistance to
become the load and can blow a fuse, damage equipment, or release dangerous energy.

At changing or high-frequency signals, a voltmeter or oscilloscope input also has
capacitance, leads have inductance, and ground paths can alter the system. Those
effects belong to F07–F08; the DC resistance is the correct first check here, not a
complete instrument description.

## Reconcile the straight line with bounded evidence

Suppose an approved, current-limited source is nominally 5.00 V. A calculation
uses $R_S=47.0~\Omega$ and predicts its terminal voltage for several known loads.
The source is also assumed to enter constant-current operation at 20.0 mA. The
piecewise teaching rule is

$$
I=\min\!\left(
\frac{V_\text{OC}}{R_S+R_L},\ I_\text{limit}
\right),
\qquad V=IR_L.
$$ {#eq-current-limit}

This rule is an approximation to one possible CV/CC supply. It does not describe
loop dynamics, foldback, hiccup protection, lead resistance, or transition error.

| $R_L$ | Linear prediction $V$ | Piecewise prediction $V$ | Synthetic observation | Region |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|---|
| open | 5.000 V | 5.000 V | 4.999 V | CV, no load |
| 10.0 kΩ | 4.977 V | 4.977 V | 4.976 V | CV |
| 1.00 kΩ | 4.776 V | 4.776 V | 4.777 V | CV |
| 330 Ω | 4.377 V | 4.377 V | 4.375 V | CV |
| 100 Ω | 3.401 V | 2.000 V | 1.998 V | CC |

: **Synthetic, illustrative data** generated to resemble rounded observations from the stated piecewise relation, with small arbitrary offsets added for diagnosis practice. They are not physical measurements and do not qualify any claim about a real supply. {#tbl-source-synthetic}

The first four rows agree with the linear calculation to a few millivolts. Using
the 4.999 V synthetic open-circuit value and the 330 Ω row gives
$I=4.375/330=13.26$ mA and

$$
R_S\approx\frac{4.999-4.375}{13.26~\text{mA}}
=47.1~\Omega.
$$

The 100 Ω row does not imply that $R_S$ suddenly became 150 Ω. Its 19.98 mA load
current, near the declared 20.0 mA limit, and the CC state discriminate a change of
operating region from a constant-resistance source fault. The disagreement is the
useful signal.

The following standard-library program reproduces the calculations and performs
the divider worst-case check used later. It is executable arithmetic, not an
independent simulation or measurement; it remains on the **calculation** rung of
the evidence ladder.

```python
# f03_operating_points.py -- reproducible F03 calculations.

def source_point(v_oc, r_source, r_load, i_limit):
    """Return terminal current, voltage, and CV/CC region."""
    i_linear = v_oc / (r_source + r_load)
    current = min(i_linear, i_limit)
    region = "CV" if i_linear <= i_limit else "CC"
    return current, current * r_load, region


def divider(v_source, r_top, r_bottom, r_load):
    """Return loaded divider voltage and source current."""
    r_parallel = r_bottom * r_load / (r_bottom + r_load)
    current = v_source / (r_top + r_parallel)
    return current * r_parallel, current


v_oc = 5.00
r_source = 47.0
i_limit = 20.0e-3

for r_load in (10_000.0, 1_000.0, 330.0, 100.0):
    current, voltage, region = source_point(
        v_oc, r_source, r_load, i_limit
    )
    print(
        f"RL={r_load:7.0f} ohm: "
        f"V={voltage:5.3f} V, "
        f"I={1e3 * current:5.2f} mA, {region}"
    )

# Worst low output: low source, high top R, low bottom R,
# minimum load, and opposing 50 ppm/K TCR over 50 K.
tcr_shift = 50e-6 * 50.0
r_top_hi = 1_000.0 * 1.01 * (1.0 + tcr_shift)
r_bottom_lo = 1_000.0 * 0.99 * (1.0 - tcr_shift)
v_low, _ = divider(4.95, r_top_hi, r_bottom_lo, 100_000.0)

# Worst high output is unloaded with the opposite extremes.
r_top_lo = 1_000.0 * 0.99 * (1.0 - tcr_shift)
r_bottom_hi = 1_000.0 * 1.01 * (1.0 + tcr_shift)
v_high = 5.05 * r_bottom_hi / (r_top_lo + r_bottom_hi)

print(f"divider worst-case: {v_low:.3f} V to {v_high:.3f} V")
```

```text
# Expected output:
# RL=  10000 ohm: V=4.977 V, I= 0.50 mA, CV
# RL=   1000 ohm: V=4.776 V, I= 4.78 mA, CV
# RL=    330 ohm: V=4.377 V, I=13.26 mA, CV
# RL=    100 ohm: V=2.000 V, I=20.00 mA, CC
# divider worst-case: 2.432 V to 2.557 V
```

A physical test would need the source identity, warm-up state, current-limit
setting, leads, load values and ratings, instruments and ranges, calibration state,
raw readings, temperature, and an uncertainty basis. Repetition could then test
whether $R_S$ stays stable. Until that is done, the table supports learning and
code verification only, not a claim about hardware.

## A resistor value is only the first selection parameter

Schematics show an exact symbol such as 1 kΩ; purchased parts have a nominal value,
a permitted initial deviation, environmental sensitivity, and limits. Marking
systems for resistors and capacitors are standardized in IEC 60062, while preferred
number series are specified in IEC 60063 [@iec60062; @iec60063]. Always verify a
part's actual manufacturer documentation when the identification matters.

### Read common colour and printed codes

For the familiar axial-resistor colour code, the digit colours are:

| Colour | Digit | Multiplier |
|---|---:|---:|
| black | 0 | $10^0$ |
| brown | 1 | $10^1$ |
| red | 2 | $10^2$ |
| orange | 3 | $10^3$ |
| yellow | 4 | $10^4$ |
| green | 5 | $10^5$ |
| blue | 6 | $10^6$ |
| violet | 7 | $10^7$ |
| grey | 8 | $10^8$ |
| white | 9 | $10^9$ |
| gold | — | $10^{-1}$ |
| silver | — | $10^{-2}$ |

: Common resistor colour digits and multipliers. IEC 60062 contains the normative coding rules, additional cases, tolerance colours, and temperature-coefficient markings [@iec60062]. {#tbl-resistor-colours}

A **four-band** code normally gives two significant digits, a multiplier, and a
tolerance. Yellow–violet–red–gold is
$47\times10^2~\Omega=4.7$ kΩ with ±5 % tolerance. A **five-band** value normally
uses three significant digits, then multiplier and tolerance:
brown–black–black–red–brown is
$100\times10^2~\Omega=10.0$ kΩ with ±1 % tolerance. A sixth band can encode a
temperature coefficient. Band spacing and the tolerance band usually indicate
reading direction, but ambiguous, heat-darkened, or nonstandard parts should be
checked with documentation and, when safe and isolated, a meter.

Common printed codes use the same significant-digits-plus-multiplier idea. “472”
means $47\times10^2=4.7$ kΩ; “1002” means
$100\times10^2=10.0$ kΩ; and a letter can replace the decimal point, so “4R7”
means 4.7 Ω. Very small parts may be unmarked, and some precision codes require a
lookup table. Do not guess from package size or body colour.

Preferred **E-series** values distribute nominal values approximately
logarithmically through each decade. For example, E12 uses
1.0, 1.2, 1.5, 1.8, 2.2, 2.7, 3.3, 3.9, 4.7, 5.6, 6.8, and 8.2 multiplied by any
power of ten. IEC 60063 defines the series and explains their relationship to
marking resolution and tolerance; membership in a series does not by itself prove
a particular part's tolerance [@iec60063].

### Tolerance, temperature, and ratings answer different questions

**Tolerance** is a guaranteed or classified interval around the nominal resistance
under the manufacturer's stated reference conditions. A 1.00 kΩ ±1 % part may
initially be anywhere from 990 Ω to 1010 Ω. Tolerance is not measurement
uncertainty, not a probability distribution, and not permission to assume that
temperature and aging cause no further change.

The **temperature coefficient of resistance** (TCR), often stated in parts per
million per kelvin, supports a first-order estimate over its specified range:

$$
R(T)\approx R(T_0)
\left[1+\alpha(T-T_0)\right].
$$ {#eq-tcr}

Here $T_0$ is the reference temperature, $\alpha$ is the signed TCR in K⁻¹, and the
approximation assumes a sufficiently linear range. A magnitude limit of
50 ppm/K over 50 K permits a resistance shift of up to
$50\times10^{-6}\times50=0.0025$, or 0.25 %, in this first-order screen.

The **rated dissipation** is not simply “power the resistor always survives.” It
depends on ambient temperature, heat flow through leads or pads, board layout,
permitted film temperature, duration, and derating. A separate **maximum operating
voltage** can dominate for high resistance values even when $V^2/R$ is below the
nominal power rating. Pulse-energy, overload, insulation, noise, voltage
coefficient, and long-term drift can matter in their own applications.

As an authentic example, the Vishay MRS25 leaded metal-film resistor datasheet
specifies a 1 Ω to 10 MΩ range, ±1 % tolerance, ±50 ppm/K TCR, rated dissipation
$P_{70}=0.6$ W, a 350 V maximum AC/DC operating voltage, and a −55 °C to 155 °C
operating range. It explicitly relates power dissipation to temperature rise and
heat flow, and requires the permitted film temperature to be respected
[@vishaymrs25]. These are **datasheet specifications for that series**, not generic
properties of every resistor. The 350 V figure lies far outside this chapter's
practical safety boundary and conveys no authorization to operate there. The same
datasheet identifies MBA/SMA parts as suitable replacements, so MRS25 is used here
as an available-part teaching example, not as an unqualified recommendation for a
new product [@vishaymrs25].

For positive steady DC under associated voltage and current references, terminal
power is $P=VI$. Substituting the ohmic relations $I=V/R$ and $V=IR$ gives the
equivalent screens

$$
P=VI=\frac{V^2}{R}=I^2R.
$$

Consequently, $P=V^2/R\le P_\text{rated}$ implies
$|V|\le\sqrt{P_\text{rated}R}$, but the permitted magnitude is the smaller of that
result and the applicable voltage rating, after derating and pulse conditions are
applied. F05 develops power, temperature rise, and margin rigorously; here the
lesson is simply that “correct resistance” is not a complete selection.

## A bounded divider design closes the loop

Design is where nominal arithmetic meets source behavior, loading, tolerance,
temperature, and measurement. Consider this deliberately modest requirement:

- source terminal voltage: 5.00 V nominal, guaranteed 4.95 V to 5.05 V under the
  divider load;
- required output: 2.50 V ±3 %, hence 2.425 V to 2.575 V;
- external input resistance: at least 100 kΩ;
- temperature: 20 °C to 70 °C;
- divider's source current: no more than 3.0 mA; and
- use two available MRS25 1.00 kΩ, ±1 %, ±50 ppm/K resistors
  [@vishaymrs25].

The source-voltage guarantee is a **requirement at the loaded terminals**, not an
unloaded label; the upstream source must independently pass it. Equal nominal
resistors give 2.50 V unloaded. Loading, low source voltage, high $R_1$, and low
$R_2$ all push the output downward. Over $\Delta T=50$ K, the worst first-order TCR
magnitude is 0.25 %. A conservative low corner is therefore

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{1,\max}&=(1.00~\text{k}\Omega)(1.01)(1.0025)
=1.0125~\text{k}\Omega,\\
R_{2,\min}&=(1.00~\text{k}\Omega)(0.99)(0.9975)
=0.9875~\text{k}\Omega,\\
R_P&=R_{2,\min}\parallel100~\text{k}\Omega
=0.9779~\text{k}\Omega,\\
V_{\text{out,min}}&=(4.95~\text{V})
\frac{R_P}{R_{1,\max}+R_P}
\approx2.432~\text{V}.
\end{aligned}
$$

The high corner occurs with the highest source voltage, low $R_1$, high $R_2$,
and no external loading:

$$
V_{\text{out,max}}=(5.05~\text{V})
\frac{1.0125}{0.9875+1.0125}
\approx2.557~\text{V}.
$$

Both lie inside 2.425–2.575 V. A conservative current screen uses high source
voltage and low resistances; including the 100 kΩ path gives about 2.6 mA, below
3.0 mA. Bounding either resistor's dissipation by $I^2R$ gives less than 7 mW,
far below the series' 0.6 W rating at 70 °C, though a final product would still
apply layout, long-term drift, overload, and environmental requirements.

The lower predicted margin is only about 7 mV. The acceptance measurement must
therefore be more discriminating than the margin. A defensible test is:

1. verify the source remains within 4.95–5.05 V from TP\_S to TP0 under the
   actual divider load and at both temperature endpoints;
2. apply a characterized 100 kΩ minimum load, then test the no-load case;
3. use a voltmeter whose $R_\text{in}\ge10$ MΩ, making its loading of this
   roughly 1 kΩ output less than 0.01 %, and whose manufacturer accuracy limit
   at 2.5 V is no wider than ±1 mV; use that limit only as an input to the
   uncertainty analysis, not as expanded uncertainty;
4. construct a decision guard band $g$ from the stated coverage and the complete
   uncertainty basis, including calibration information, accuracy limits,
   resolution, repeatability, temperature stabilization, and knowledge of the
   applied load; require $g\le2$ mV for this test;
5. accept only when
   $V_\text{meas}-g>2.425$ V and $V_\text{meas}+g<2.575$ V; equality at either
   strict boundary does not pass; and
6. record resistor identities, actual resistance observations, temperature,
   source voltage, load, instrument/range, calibration state, and raw readings.

The calculation establishes a **worst-case feasibility claim under stated
independent bounds**. It does not establish production yield, long-term stability,
or physical compliance. Those require measurements and, at higher stakes,
repeated test and independent reproduction. If the complete 2 mV guard-band
requirement or temperature stabilization cannot be achieved, the present
acceptance plan is not capable of protecting the roughly 7 mV lower design margin;
the correct decision is to increase design margin or improve the test, not to
round the result into passing.

## Recognize the boundaries before they become faults

The compact relations in this chapter are powerful because their boundaries are
visible. Common breakdowns are diagnostic clues:

- **Current limit or compliance:** a source follows one straight terminal relation
  only until its regulator reaches a current, voltage, thermal, or input-energy
  limit. A mode indicator and a slope change across several loads discriminate
  this from one fixed $R_S$.
- **Self-heating:** $V=RI$ with constant $R$ fails if dissipation changes the
  element's temperature enough to change $R$. A lamp filament is a familiar
  nonlinear example. Measure only after defining whether the desired point is cold,
  transient, or thermally settled.
- **Contact and lead resistance:** these add series drops, especially in low-ohm or
  high-current work. A two-wire resistance observation includes leads and contacts;
  a four-terminal method separates the current path from the voltage-sense path.
- **Leakage and surface contamination:** a supposedly open parallel path can be
  comparable to a high-value resistor, especially with humidity or residue. The
  parallel-conductance view predicts the resulting low reading.
- **Frequency and switching:** parasitic capacitance and inductance make terminal
  relations depend on time and frequency. DC resistance alone cannot predict a fast
  edge; F07–F08 add stored energy and impedance.
- **Instrument range and reference error:** a DMM may change input resistance or
  burden between ranges. Reversed leads change the sign; a shared or earth-referred
  return can change the physical circuit. Record the connection, range, and
  reference points rather than only the display.
- **Misread or damaged components:** a wrong decade, ambiguous colour direction,
  in-circuit parallel path, or heat-damaged resistor can imitate a source fault.
  De-energize, isolate one terminal when appropriate, and compare identification,
  circuit context, and resistance evidence.

A single loaded measurement rarely identifies the cause. Several deliberately
chosen loads reveal a line, a knee, or inconsistency. That is the diagnostic value
of the operating-point view: it turns “the voltage is wrong” into competing,
testable terminal relations.

## What to carry forward

A source and load do not negotiate vaguely; they settle at the one voltage and
current that satisfy both terminal descriptions. Resistance turns field behavior
into a bounded linear relation, series paths add resistance, parallel paths add
conductance, and a finite source resistance makes load current visible as voltage
sag. Instruments are additional loads, so a reading belongs to the source–load–
instrument system unless loading has been shown negligible or corrected.

The durable working sequence is:

1. declare voltage polarity, current direction, DC or other operating condition,
   and the safety boundary;
2. predict the operating region and order of magnitude;
3. derive the source and load relations, then solve their common point;
4. include tolerances, ratings, temperature, wiring, and instrument resistance far
   enough to compare with the requirement;
5. measure at safe, discriminating operating points and reconcile disagreement;
6. state only the claim supported by the achieved evidence rung.

F04 generalizes the reductions into Kirchhoff analysis and Thévenin/Norton
equivalents. F05 follows the same operating point into energy, power, and heat.
F06 turns loading, uncertainty, and competing fault hypotheses into a systematic
measurement discipline. The simple source–resistor–load loop is therefore not a
toy to discard; it is the smallest complete example of how the book reasons about
systems.

## Exercises

Use declared references, SI units, an estimate before calculation, and a statement
of assumptions. Full solutions and live-assessment variants remain outside the
public book; selected self-check results are included for calibration.

**Quick check (multiple choice)**

One best answer each; the key follows.

1. A real 5 V source reads 4.8 V under load. The best first interpretation is:
    a. the load consumed 0.2 V;
    b. source and load settled at an operating point with 4.8 V at their terminals;
    c. Ohm's law has failed;
    d. every 5 V source is actually a 4.8 V source.
2. For positive resistors in parallel, the equivalent resistance is:
    a. greater than the largest branch;
    b. their arithmetic sum;
    c. smaller than the smallest branch;
    d. always zero.
3. An ideal voltage source has:
    a. zero source resistance and unbounded current capability;
    b. infinite source resistance and zero current;
    c. a resistance equal to its load;
    d. a fixed current and arbitrary voltage.
4. A 10 MΩ voltmeter measures a port whose output resistance is 1.0 MΩ. Its DC
   loading error relative to open circuit is approximately:
    a. 0 %;
    b. −9.1 %;
    c. +10 %;
    d. −50 %.
5. A supply changes from CV to CC as load resistance falls. This most directly
   indicates:
    a. the meter reversed polarity;
    b. the load became an open circuit;
    c. the source reached its programmed current limit;
    d. resistance can no longer be measured in ohms.
6. A resistor marked 1.00 kΩ ±1 % is guaranteed, under the stated reference
   conditions, to lie initially in:
    a. 999–1001 Ω;
    b. 990–1010 Ω;
    c. exactly 1000 Ω;
    d. any range allowed by the power rating.

*[Key: 1-b, 2-c, 3-a, 4-b, 5-c, 6-b.]*

**Retrieval and explanation**

1. Define voltage source, current source, load, resistance, resistivity,
   conductance, operating point, source resistance, and compliance voltage. For
   each source idealization, name the unphysical unlimited capability.
2. Explain why a rechargeable cell is not permanently a source or permanently a
   load. Use declared current direction and the sign of terminal power.
3. State the assumptions required to derive $R=\rho L/A$. Give one physical
   observation that would challenge each of two assumptions.
4. Explain why $V/I$ at one operating point does not prove that a device obeys a
   constant-resistance Ohm's law.

**Estimation**

5. A 3.3 V output has $R_S\approx22~\Omega$. Estimate the voltage with loads of
   10 kΩ, 220 Ω, and 22 Ω before calculating exactly. Which load makes the
   output half its open-circuit value? *[Self-check: approximately 3.29 V,
   3.00 V, and 1.65 V.]*
6. Without exact arithmetic, rank these from smallest to largest equivalent
   resistance: (a) two 1 kΩ resistors in series; (b) one 1 kΩ resistor; (c) two
   1 kΩ resistors in parallel; (d) 1 kΩ in parallel with 10 kΩ.
7. A 2.5 V node has 200 kΩ output resistance. Estimate the minimum voltmeter input
   resistance required for less than 1 % loading, then use
   @eq-meter-requirement to calculate it.

**Derivation and calculation**

8. Starting from $\vec J=\sigma\vec E$, derive $V=RI$ and
   $R=\rho L/A$ for a uniform specimen. Declare the axis, polarity, and current
   reference, and show the dimensional check.
9. Derive @eq-source-load from @eq-source-line and @eq-load-line. Then reverse the
   current reference without changing the physical circuit and write both terminal
   relations consistently.
10. Derive @eq-series and @eq-parallel using the voltage and current conservation
    statements. Prove that the parallel equivalent of positive finite resistors is
    smaller than the smallest branch.
11. A 12.0 V source reads 11.7 V at 30.0 mA and 11.1 V at 90.0 mA. Estimate
    $R_S$ and $V_\text{OC}$ using @eq-source-resistance. Predict the terminal
    voltage at 50 mA, provided the same region holds. *[Self-check:
    $R_S=10.0~\Omega$, $V_\text{OC}=12.0$ V, $V=11.5$ V.]*
12. Derive @eq-divider-loading directly from @eq-loaded-divider. Identify the
    limits $R_L\to\infty$, $R_L=R_\text{out}$, and $R_L\to0$.

**Tolerance and data interpretation**

13. A 4.7 kΩ ±5 % resistor is in series with a 10.0 kΩ ±1 % resistor. Find the
    guaranteed minimum and maximum series resistance. State why the interval is not
    a standard uncertainty.
14. Decode these common markings: (a) yellow–violet–red–gold; (b)
    brown–black–black–brown–brown; (c) “332”; (d) “4R7.” Then state what the
    marking does **not** tell you about dissipation or temperature.
15. Recalculate the apparent $R_S$ for every loaded row of
    @tbl-source-synthetic using 4.999 V as the open-circuit value. Plot or tabulate
    $V$ against $I$. Which row falsifies the single straight-line relation, and
    which recorded state explains it?
16. A real test gives $(V_1,I_1)=(4.950~\text{V},1.00~\text{mA})$ and
    $(V_2,I_2)=(4.900~\text{V},2.00~\text{mA})$, with ±5 mV voltage accuracy
    limits and ±0.02 mA current accuracy limits. Estimate $R_S$, then explain why
    the uncertainty may be too large for a strong slope claim. Choose a wider but
    still safe current separation.

**Debugging**

17. A divider built from two isolated, meter-checked 1.00 MΩ resistors reads
    4.29 V instead of 4.50 V on a 10 MΩ DMM. Show whether loading explains the
    observation before accusing either resistor. Propose a second measurement that
    discriminates loading from a wrong source voltage.
18. A nominal 5 V source follows a 47 Ω slope down to 330 Ω, but a 100 Ω load
    produces 2.0 V and lights the CC indicator. A colleague replaces the source,
    calling the first one defective. Explain why that diagnosis is unsupported and
    state one test that would support or falsify the current-limit hypothesis.
19. A 10 Ω resistor appears to measure 10.8 Ω with ordinary two-wire probes but
    10.0 Ω after the probe tips are shorted and their 0.8 Ω reading is subtracted.
    Identify the omitted series elements. Explain why simple subtraction is only a
    correction, not a four-terminal measurement.
20. An axial resistor's bands are darkened and ambiguous after overheating. Give a
    safe, de-energized identification procedure and explain why measuring it in
    circuit can read low.

**Open design**

21. Design a divider that produces nominally 1.65 V from a loaded 3.30 V source.
    The receiver input is guaranteed above 50 kΩ, divider current must be below
    1.0 mA, available resistors are E12 ±5 %, and output must remain within ±5 %
    over source and component tolerances that you must state. Determine whether a
    passive design is feasible; if not, identify the requirement that must change
    or the need for a buffer.
22. Write a safe test plan to estimate the source resistance of an unknown but
    approved extra-low-voltage output without shorting it. Specify load choices,
    ratings, instruments, references, stop conditions, calculations, uncertainty,
    and the evidence needed before calling the source linear over the tested range.
23. Select a real resistor series for a 1.0 kΩ load expected to dissipate 0.20 W
    at 60 °C ambient. Use a current manufacturer datasheet. Check resistance range,
    tolerance, TCR, derating, voltage, steady dissipation, overload or pulse needs,
    and layout conditions. State exactly what your document review establishes and
    what still requires physical qualification.

## Connections

Chapter ID: `F03`. Its prerequisites are [F02](f02-charge-voltage-current.qmd)
(charge, voltage, current, nodes, and reference directions) and
[M01](../appendices/m01-algebra-units-complex.qmd) (algebra, ratios, graphs, and SI
units), as recorded in [`chapters.toml`](../chapters.toml).

This chapter is the middle of the Part I **measurement spine**
F01 → F02 → F03 → F06. It hands operating-point and loading reasoning to
[F04](f04-networks-kirchhoff.qmd), power and resistor-rating consequences to
[F05](f05-energy-power-thermal.qmd), and measurement intrusion plus fault
discrimination to [F06](f06-measurement-uncertainty-debug.qmd). The aligned
practical evidence is **Lab L02 — Source, load, resistance, and meter loading**;
the ideas also feed integrating milestone **M1**. These IDs denote learning and
evidence goals, not authorization to energize hardware. The
[reading roadmap](../roadmap.qmd) and [Part I introduction](index.qmd) show the
full route.

## References

Claims are cited where used. The physical and circuit treatment rests on canonical
electronics and device references [@horowitz2015art; @sedra2020microelectronic];
marking codes and preferred resistor values on the relevant IEC standards
[@iec60062; @iec60063]; and the worked component-rating case on the cited
MRS16/MRS25 manufacturer datasheet [@vishaymrs25]. SI and uncertainty language
follows the references established in F01 [@bipm2019si; @jcgm2008gum]. The complete
[bibliography](../references.qmd) collects these sources. A standard, datasheet, or
chapter never substitutes for local authorization, a complete application review,
or physical qualification.
